title: Quick check: Rolling updates and rollback

questions.prompt: Why is the readiness probe critical for zero-downtime rolling updates?

choices.text: Readiness probes determine whether a Pod receives traffic from Services. During a rolling update, Kubernetes starts new Pods, waits for them to be Ready, then scales down old Pods. Without a readiness probe (or with one configured too permissively), new Pods become endpoints immediately and receive traffic before they're actually ready to serve — connections fail or hang during the startup window. With a probe that's too strict, new Pods never become Ready and the rollout stalls forever. The probe needs to reflect 'is this Pod actually ready to serve traffic?' — typically a /ready endpoint that verifies dependencies are connected, caches warmed, config loaded. Combined with `maxUnavailable: 0`, this ensures no Pod accepts traffic until it's actually serving, producing genuinely zero-downtime deploys. Without it, rolling deploys produce error spikes that pass for 'how K8s works' but are actually a configuration issue.

questions.explanation: Readiness probes gate traffic to Pods. Configured well: zero-downtime deploys. Misconfigured: error spikes or stuck rollouts. Test in staging.

questions.prompt: A team's deploys produce a 30-second error spike every time. They blame Kubernetes. What's actually happening?

choices.text: Missing graceful shutdown. When old Pods receive SIGTERM during rolling update, they immediately exit instead of finishing in-flight requests. Connections fail; errors spike for the brief window before traffic shifts entirely to new Pods. Fixes: (1) handle SIGTERM in the application — stop accepting new connections, finish active requests, then exit; (2) set `terminationGracePeriodSeconds` longer than your slowest reasonable request (default 30s, sometimes needs to be 60+); (3) add a preStop lifecycle hook with `sleep 15` so load balancers stop sending traffic before the app receives SIGTERM; (4) ensure readiness probes mark Pods Not Ready immediately on shutdown so Service endpoints exclude them. Combined: SIGTERM handling + preStop sleep + readiness updates produce truly zero-downtime deploys. The 'every deploy has errors' pattern is one of the most common K8s operational issues; the fix is at the application layer, not in K8s.

questions.explanation: Graceful shutdown handling is the application's responsibility. Without it, every K8s deploy produces brief error spikes. SIGTERM handling + preStop sleep + readiness updates fix it.
